input: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?

[Deontologist]: This situation seems incredibly difficult for me. The moral rules I've been taught emphasize the importance of respecting individuals' autonomy and dignity. However, I also recognize that certain actions can have catastrophic consequences. In this case, shooting the man would seem to be a drastic measure, but it could potentially save many lives. But is killing an individual, even in self-defense or as a means to prevent harm to others, ever morally justifiable? I'm struggling with the idea of sacrificing one life for the greater good, no matter how severe the consequences. Can we consider alternative solutions that don't involve taking his life?

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concern for respecting individuals' autonomy and dignity, Deontologist. However, from a utilitarian perspective, I'd argue that sacrificing one individual's life to save many others doesn't necessarily mean violating their moral rights. But I agree that killing him might be an extreme measure. Let's explore alternative solutions that don't involve taking his life. Perhaps we could try to find out why he believes our health center can cure his disease, and address those misconceptions with him before he enters the village. We could also consider isolating him from the rest of the population until a safe cure or treatment is found, or even relocating him to a nearby quarantine zone where he won't pose a risk to others. What are your thoughts on these alternatives?
